title: Design a Global ERP Platform
description: Requirements (100K+ users, 50 countries, multi-currency, multi-GAAP, real-time consolidation), data model, storage (HANA column store, partitioning), microservices vs modular monolith, event-driven integration, extensibility, tradeoff analysis.

description.value:
Designing a global ERP platform is one of the most complex system design challenges a Staff+ candidate can face. It touches every aspect of distributed systems: data modeling at scale, multi-region consistency, regulatory compliance, extensibility, and real-time processing. This question has appeared in Google L6 and Meta E6 interviews for enterprise-focused roles.

Functional Requirements: (1) Financial Management — General Ledger, Accounts Payable/Receivable, fixed assets, multi-GAAP (IFRS, US GAAP, local GAAP) with automated consolidation across 50+ entities. (2) Supply Chain — Procurement, inventory management, order-to-cash, plan-to-produce across global factories and warehouses. (3) Human Capital — Payroll (country-specific rules), time tracking, benefits administration. (4) Real-Time Consolidation — Group close within 24 hours across 50 countries with automatic currency translation (50+ currencies) and intercompany elimination.

Non-Functional Requirements: (1) Scale: 100,000+ concurrent users, 500+ transactions per second at peak, 10 TB+ of financial data. (2) Availability: 99.99% uptime (52 minutes/year downtime max) during financial close periods. (3) Latency: 95th percentile response time under 500ms for transactional screens, under 3 seconds for reporting queries. (4) Compliance: SOX, GDPR, local data residency (data must stay in-country for certain jurisdictions), audit trail for all financial transactions. (5) Multi-tenancy: Support for 50 country-specific legal entities with separate chart of accounts, currencies, and fiscal year variants.

The functional decomposition follows SAP's modular architecture: each domain (FI, CO, MM, SD, HCM) is a bounded context with well-defined APIs. However, unlike SAP's tightly coupled architecture, our design must allow independent deployability, independent scaling, and technology heterogeneity where justified.

ambiguityScaffold.first60seconds: "Before I draw anything, let me scope this ERP platform. Three clarifying questions: (1) What modules are in scope — Finance, Supply Chain, HCM? (2) Is this replacing existing SAP ECC or building greenfield? (3) Do we need multi-GAAP and multi-currency? While you answer those, I will start with the data model because it is the hardest constraint and everything else follows. My assumption: 50 legal entities across 30 countries, multi-GAAP required, 20M transactions/day peak during month-end close."
assumptions: 50 legal entities across 30 countries with different regulatory requirements
Multi-GAAP required (US GAAP + IFRS + local GAAP per entity)
20M transactions/day peak during Friday close event (~230 TPS)
Existing SAP ECC systems exist — this is a migration, not greenfield
99.95% uptime SLA (no more than 21 minutes downtime/month)
ambiguityScaffold.deferred: I am deferring HCM and Procurement module details to keep the scope focused. Initial design addresses Finance and Supply Chain — the hardest constraints. Integration points for deferred modules will be sketched if time permits.

description.value:
The data model is the heart of an ERP system. A global ERP must model legal entities, chart of accounts, currencies, fiscal periods, and intercompany relationships while maintaining referential integrity across 50 countries with different regulatory requirements.

Core Entities: The foundational entity is Legal Entity (Company Code in SAP terms), which owns all transactions and determines which GAAP applies. Each Legal Entity has: a chart of accounts (which can be group-wide or country-specific), a functional currency, a fiscal year variant (calendar year vs. April–March), and local regulatory requirements. Above Legal Entity is the Group/Controlling Area, which consolidates multiple legal entities for group reporting.

General Ledger Design: The GL is modeled as an append-only ledger with line items (not balances). Each line item has: Legal Entity, Account, Currency, Amount (in transaction currency and group currency), Period, Year, and dimensions (Cost Center, Profit Center, Segment, Product). Balances are derived by aggregating line items — never stored directly. This is the event sourcing pattern: the ledger is the event log, and the balance is the materialized view. SAP S/4HANA uses the same approach with the ACDOCA table (Universal Journal).

Multi-Currency & Multi-GAAP: Each transaction is stored with the original transaction currency, the local currency of the legal entity, and the group currency. Currency translation rates are time-series data stored with validity periods. For multi-GAAP, each line item has a ledger group identifier: IFRS, US GAAP, Local GAAP, and Tax. Differences between GAAP treatments are stored as parallel valuations on the same line item. SAP S/4HANA supports this via the Leading Ledger + Non-Leading Ledger model. In our design, this is implemented as a ledger dimension on each journal entry.

Intercompany Design: Intercompany transactions (e.g., Legal Entity A sells to Legal Entity B) must be recorded in both entities with offsetting entries that always balance. The design uses a dual-entry with pairing pattern: each intercompany transaction generates two journal entries (one per entity) linked by a common Intercompany ID. The consolidation engine matches these pairs and eliminates intercompany balances automatically. The challenge is ensuring that both entries are recorded atomically — this requires a distributed transaction pattern (Saga with compensating transactions).

decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is this entity append-only (event log) or mutable (master data)?" Append-only entities like journal entries use event sourcing with ACDOCA-style ledger tables. Mutable entities like customer master use normalized relational models with audit trails.

branches.condition: Append-only transactional data (journal entries, line items)
branches.path: Event sourcing model: append-only ledger table derived from ACDOCA pattern. Balances are materialized views, never stored directly. Enables multi-GAAP parallel valuations and point-in-time queries.

branches.condition: Mutable master data with frequent updates (customer, vendor, material)
branches.path: Normalized relational model with change history table. All updates create a new version record. Use CDS views for the active version. Enables audit trail and temporal queries.

branches.condition: Highly relational data with cross-entity constraints (order-to-cash)
branches.path: Hybrid: relational for transactional integrity (foreign keys, ACID), event-sourced for audit. SAP's Universal Journal (ACDOCA) approach: store the transaction once with all dimensions.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The data model decision is the most consequential for an ERP. Get it wrong and every downstream system suffers. Show the interviewer you understand that the General Ledger is an event log, not a balance sheet — the event sourcing pattern is the right mental model, and ACDOCA proves it at billion-row scale.

sections.title: Storage Architecture — HANA Column Store & Partitioning

description.value:
Storage is the critical bottleneck in global ERP systems. A single financial table (like SAP's ACDOCA) can contain 2–5 billion rows in a large enterprise. Query performance requires a combination of column-store compression, intelligent partitioning, and tiered storage.

Column Store vs. Row Store: SAP HANA's column store is the default choice for transactional + analytical workloads (HTAP). Columnar storage provides 10–20x compression on financial data (repeated chart-of-account values compress well) and enables vectorized aggregation for reporting queries. Row store is appropriate for: configuration tables (small, frequently updated row-by-row), user session data, and tables requiring single-row lookups by primary key. In our design, transactional fact tables (Journal Entry, Line Item) use column store with partitioning; small dimension tables use row store.

Partitioning Strategy: The primary partitioning key is by Legal Entity + Fiscal Period. This enables partition pruning for the most common query pattern: "Show me all entries for Company Code X in Period Y." Each partition is 100–500 GB depending on the entity size. Sub-partitioning by Ledger Group (IFRS vs US GAAP vs Local) further improves query performance. Range partitioning on the posting date is used for time-series queries. The partitioning scheme must be designed with a maximum partition count that the HANA instance can manage efficiently (typically < 5000 partitions per table).

Data Tiering (Hot/Warm/Cold): We implement a three-tier storage strategy. Hot tier: Current fiscal year + prior year on HANA in-memory (fastest, most expensive). Warm tier: Prior 5 years on HANA native storage extension (NSE) — disk-based but HANA-optimized, at 10% of in-memory cost. Cold tier: Older data in object store (S3/ADLS) accessible via HANA SQL on files or via data lake queries (Spark/Presto). The data movement between tiers is automatic based on configurable retention policies and is transparent to applications via a virtualization layer.

Indexing: Column store tables use HANA's built-in indexes (inverted indices for text, bitmap for low-cardinality columns). Additional secondary indexes on foreign keys (Legal Entity, Account, Cost Center) speed up join queries. The most critical optimization is the pre-aggregation layer — materialized aggregate tables (HANA Aggregation Views) that pre-summarize data by common dimension hierarchies (Entity → Region → Group, Account → Account Group → Financial Statement Item).

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 380" 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.5}50%{opacity:1}}</style><rect class="st-box" x="15" y="15" width="670" height="350"/><text class="st-lbl" x="350" y="38">Data Tiering & Fiscal Year)</text><text class="st-sub" x="350" y="92">Partitioned by Legal Entity + Period | Sub-second queries | Highest cost/GB</text><rect class="st-tier" x="40" y="115" width="620" height="50" Years)</text><text class="st-sub" x="350" y="212">Parquet format | Spark/Presto queries | 15-year retention for compliance</text><rect class="st-box" x="40" y="245" width="300" height="85"/><text Group</text><text class="st-sub" x="190" y="313">Range: Posting Date</text><text class="st-sub" x="190" y="328">Max: 5000 partitions per table</text><rect class="st-box" x="360" y="245" width="300" height="85"/><text class="st-lbl" x="510" y="265">Pre-Aggregation Layer</text><text class="st-sub" x="510" y="283">CDS Aggregation Views</text><text class="st-sub" x="510" y="298">Materialized aggregates</text><text class="st-sub" x="510" y="313">Dimension hierarchies</text><text class="st-sub" x="510" y="328">Sub-second reporting queries</text></svg>

calculation.formula: MonthlyStorageCost = HotGB x $45 + WarmGB x $4.50 + ColdGB x $0.023

ap.monthlyPenalty: $23,000 all on NSE (10 TB x $2.30) but query latency degrades 25x for the hot dataset

calculation.breakeven: At 5+ TB, three-tier tiering saves 60% vs all-in-memory while keeping the hot working set at in-memory speed. The breakeven point is at 2 TB hot data — above that, NSE becomes mandatory regardless of budget.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your three-tier storage adds operational complexity. Why not just scale HANA vertically with more memory? SAP recommends all-in-memory for performance."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "SAP recommends all-in-memory because they sell HANA licenses." (dismissive, doesn't address the engineering tradeoff)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "That is a valid concern about operational complexity. Let me address the cost-performance calculus. An all-in-memory approach for 10 TB requires a HANA instance with 10 TB RAM — at $45/GB/month, that is $450K/month. Three-tier storage costs $117K/month — a 74% reduction. The tradeoff: cold data queries (data older than 6 years) take 5-15 seconds instead of 100ms. For regulatory queries and audits, that latency is acceptable — these are not user-facing operations. The operational complexity is managed by automation: data movement between tiers is handled by HANA's native Data Tiering Optimization (DTO) with configurable policies. The system automatically moves data between hot, warm, and cold tiers based on access patterns. The team does not manually manage tiering — they set policies. The real question is: do we save $333K/month and accept 5-second queries on 50% of our data, or spend $450K/month for 100ms queries on archival data? For most enterprises, the 74% cost savings far outweigh the cold-tier query latency."
adversarialResponse.pattern: quantify-and-compare — accept the valid concern, provide specific cost numbers that demonstrate the savings, explain the automated management that reduces operational burden, and reframe the debate as a business decision with clear tradeoffs.

description.value:
The architectural pattern for a global ERP platform is a topic of intense debate. SAP has moved from the monolithic R/3 architecture to the modular S/4HANA with extensibility via BTP. FAANG interviewers will expect you to analyze the tradeoffs between microservices and modular monolith for an ERP context.

Modular Monolith (Recommended): A modular monolith is a single deployment unit with well-defined module boundaries. Each module (Finance, Supply Chain, HCM) has its own schema, API surface, and data model, but all modules run in the same process and share the same database instance. Benefits: (1) Transactional consistency — cross-module transactions (e.g., creating a sales order that updates inventory and financials) can be truly ACID without Saga complexity. (2) Operational simplicity — one deployment, one monitoring stack, one team to manage infrastructure. (3) Query performance — joins across modules don't require network calls. The tradeoff is that modules cannot be deployed independently, scaling is all-or-nothing, and teams must coordinate on schema changes.

Microservices (Not Recommended for Core ERP): Each module is independently deployed, with its own database and API. Benefits: independent scaling, independent deployability, technology heterogeneity. However, the costs are severe for ERP: (1) Distributed transactions — a single business process (Order-to-Cash) touches 5+ services, requiring Saga patterns with compensating transactions. This adds enormous complexity. (2) Consistency latency — eventual consistency across services means the financial close can't happen in real-time. (3) Join complexity — reports requiring data from 5+ services need fan-out queries with in-memory merge, which breaks at scale.

Hybrid Approach: The practical recommendation is modular monolith for core ERP (Finance, Supply Chain, HCM core) and microservices for edge capabilities. Edge services that benefit from independent scaling include: Document Management, Reporting/Analytics, Workflow Engine, Integration Gateway, and AI/ML services. These edge services communicate with the core via async events and materialized view caches, not synchronous API calls.

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 380" 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.5}50%{opacity:1}}</style><rect class="arch-box" x="15" y="15" width="670" height="350"/><text class="arch-lbl" x="350" y="35">Modular Monolith vs Microservices</text><rect class="arch-mod" x="20" y="50" width="310" height="180" stroke="var(--svg-success-stroke)"/><text class="arch-lbl" x="175" y="70">Modular Monolith (Core ERP)</text><text cross-module txn</text><text class="arch-sub" x="525" y="158">- Eventual consistency delays close</text><text class="arch-sub" x="525" y="174">- Fan-out query performance issues</text><text class="arch-sub" x="525" y="194">USE FOR: Document Mgmt, Analytics, AI</text><rect class="arch-box" x="20" y="250" width="660" height="80"/><text class="arch-lbl" x="350" y="270">Hybrid Architecture</text><text class="arch-sub" x="350" y="290">Core ERP (Modular Monolith) ← Event Bus → Edge Services (Microservices)</text><text class="arch-sub" x="350" y="308">Core owns the data + transactions. Edge services own capabilities that need independent scaling.</text></svg>

decisionTree.firstQuestion: "How many independent deployment cadences do you need?" If the answer is 1-2, start with a modular monolith. If 5+ independent teams need to deploy on different schedules, the coordination cost of a monolith exceeds the operational cost of microservices.

branches.condition: Fewer than 3 teams, single deployment cadence, strong consistency needs
branches.path: Modular monolith for core ERP (FI, CO, MM, SD, HCM). Clear module boundaries with strict APIs. ACID transactions across modules. Cheaper operations: 1 deployment, 1 monitoring stack.

branches.condition: 5+ independent teams with different deploy schedules
branches.path: Microservices for edge capabilities only (document management, analytics, workflow). Each service owns its data store. Cost: 3x operational overhead for service mesh + observability.

branches.condition: Organization growing from 2 to 10 teams over 18 months
branches.path: Start modular monolith with well-defined bounded contexts (DDD). Plan extraction path upfront — each module has API contracts and independent data schemas. Extract one service per quarter when team boundaries solidify.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: At Staff+, your recommendation must consider organizational maturity. Microservices are an organizational pattern, not a technical one. Conway's Law applies: your service boundaries will mirror your team boundaries. Design for the org you expect in 18 months.

organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: The 'Squeeze and Strangle' pattern: identify bounded contexts using event storming with all 5 engineering teams (4-week workshop). Extract one bounded context per quarter starting with the most independently deployable module (usually reporting or notifications). Each extraction gets its own CI/CD pipeline and database schema.

stakeholders.concern: Module extraction creates new services = new on-call burden
stakeholders.address: Each extracted service gets its own on-call rotation. The monolith team's on-call load decreases proportionally to lines extracted. Net change is zero per team during transition.
Each extraction includes a feature improvement for the extracted module — an API endpoint the product team was already requesting. Extraction is never pure refactoring; it always ships user value.

Parallel extraction: two bounded contexts extracted simultaneously by independent teams. Timeline goes from 12 to 6 months. Risk: requires 2 senior architects simultaneously — acceptable if available.

description.value:
Event-driven architecture is the nervous system of a global ERP platform. Every business event (order created, invoice posted, material moved) generates events that flow to dependent systems — analytics, compliance, external partners, and edge services. The integration design must handle 500+ events per second with exactly-once semantics and sub-100ms latency.

Event Taxonomy: Events are categorized into three types. Domain Events represent business facts: "OrderPlaced," "InvoicePosted," "GoodsReceived." These are emitted by the core ERP modules and are immutable — they represent something that happened, not a command. Integration Events are derived from domain events for external consumption: they may contain transformed data, enriched with additional context, and formatted for specific consumers. Command Events request an action from a downstream service (e.g., "SendEmail," "TriggerApprovalWorkflow"). Only domain events go on the main event bus; integration events and commands are produced by event processors.

Schema Registry & Evolution: Every event has a schema registered in the Schema Registry (Avro or CloudEvents schema). The schema includes the event type, version (semver), producer, timestamp, and payload. Schema evolution must be backward-compatible: new consumers can read old events, and old consumers can skip unknown fields. This is critical for a 15-year ERP lifecycle where events outlast the services that produced them.

Event Processing Pipelines: Events flow through processing pipelines implemented as Kafka Streams or Flink jobs. Examples: (1) Currency Translation Pipeline — reads "InvoicePosted" events, applies the exchange rate valid at the posting date, writes the translated event to the analytics topic. (2) Intercompany Matching Pipeline — pairs intercompany events from different legal entities, identifies unmatched entries, and alerts the reconciliation team. (3) Real-Time Consolidation Pipeline — aggregates events from all entities into group-level materialized views, enabling the 24-hour close.

Dead Letter Queue & Retry: Failed events (due to schema mismatch, validation failure, or downstream unavailability) go to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ). The DLQ has a configurable retry policy: 3 retries with exponential backoff (10s, 60s, 300s), then manual intervention via an alerting dashboard. This pattern is essential for SAP integration reliability — a single failed event should never block the processing of subsequent events.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your event-driven architecture adds 5-second end-to-end latency. For financial close, we need sub-second consistency. Why not use synchronous API calls instead?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Event-driven architecture is the industry standard for ERP integration." (defensive, no acknowledgment of the real constraint)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You are right to flag the latency concern for financial close. Let me clarify the architecture: we use a Command-Query Separation pattern. The financial close process uses synchronous API calls for the core ledger update — that is the command path, which runs at sub-100ms P99 latency. The event-driven path is for downstream consumers: analytics, reporting, compliance monitoring. Those consumers can tolerate 1-5 second latency because they are not in the user-facing transaction flow. The events are produced via the transactional outbox pattern — the event is published as part of the same database transaction as the ledger update. This means the event is guaranteed to be published exactly once, and the downstream consumer sees it within 1-5 seconds. The core financial close still works synchronously. The events enable use cases that synchronous APIs cannot: multiple consumers without fan-out, replay for error recovery, and cross-system audit trails. If the requirement is truly sub-second end-to-end for ALL consumers, we can use Kafka with min.insync.replicas=2 and acks=all to achieve sub-100ms broker acknowledgment, but the consumer processing time depends on the specific consumer's workload."
adversarialResponse.pattern: clarify-and-separate — acknowledge the challenge, clarify that the architecture is not purely async but uses command-query separation, describe the specific pattern (transactional outbox) that guarantees exactly-once delivery, and quantify the latency per path.

description.value:
Extensibility is the hardest design challenge in a global ERP platform. Each of the 50 legal entities has unique requirements: local accounting rules, country-specific tax calculations, custom approval workflows, and industry-specific fields. The platform must support customization without creating the 3,200 Z-program nightmare that SAP customers face today.

Extension Points: We design three layers of extensibility. Layer 1: Configuration (no-code) — fields, rules, and workflows that can be configured via UI without code changes. This covers 70% of customization needs: chart of accounts, tax codes, approval hierarchies, fiscal year variants, and reporting layouts. Layer 2: Low-Code Extensions — CDS View extensions, custom fields via app (CFA), and business rules via SAP Business Rules Framework + DMN. This covers 20% of needs. Layer 3: Pro-Code Extensions — custom BAdIs (from software, not classic), custom APIs, and event handlers. This is reserved for the remaining 10% and requires architecture review.

The Extensibility Contract: Every extension point has a defined contract: what is the input (context, parameters), what is the expected output (result, side effects), and what are the constraints (execution time limit, read/write permissions, upgrade compatibility). Extensions that violate the contract are rejected at deployment time. This is enforced by a Static Analysis Gate that checks: no direct table access (use CDS views only), no modification of SAP standard objects, no synchronous cross-entity calls, and no blocking operations.

Multi-Tenancy Model: We use a hybrid tenancy model. The Data Plane is entity-isolated — each legal entity's data is stored in separate partitions (same database, isolated by partition key). The Control Plane (user management, configuration, extension deployment) is shared. This gives us operational efficiency (single database to manage) while maintaining data isolation (an entity cannot access another entity's data without explicit cross-entity authorization). The Extension Deployment is tenant-aware: extensions can be deployed to specific entities, all entities, or entity groups.

class="ext-sub" x="515" y="293">Data isolated by partition key</text><text class="ext-sub" x="515" y="308">Shared control plane | Tenant-aware deploys</text></svg>

organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Implement the 70-20-10 extensibility framework with a phased rollout. Phase 1: deploy the configuration UI (Layer 1) to all 50 entities — this covers 70% of needs immediately. Phase 2: enable CDS view extensions (Layer 2) for the 10 most complex entities. Phase 3: establish the Pro-Code governance board for Layer 3 extensions with architecture review and static analysis gates.

stakeholders.concern: Loss of control over local customizations. Our local tax requirements are unique and won't be covered by global configuration.
stakeholders.address: Each country gets a dedicated extension namespace within the 70-20-10 framework. Layer 2 extensions (CDS views, custom fields) are self-service — no global change request needed. Only Layer 3 (pro-code) requires architecture review, and that has a 48-hour SLA.

stakeholders.concern: Extensions will break during upgrades. We have seen this with Z-programs.
stakeholders.address: Layer 1 and 2 extensions are upgrade-safe by design — they use CDS extension views and custom fields that survive upgrades. Layer 3 extensions require contract enforcement: no direct table access, no SAP object modification. Static analysis gates run in CI and block non-compliant extensions.

stakeholders.concern: Too much flexibility means inconsistent processes across entities. We need standardization.
stakeholders.address: The framework enforces a 'configure within boundaries' model. Each process has a configurable parameter space — countries can adjust within defined limits (e.g., tax rates, approval thresholds) but cannot change the process flow. Process standardization is enforced at Layer 0 (the core process definition). Local variation is permitted only within the parameter space.

description.value:
In a FAANG system design interview, the tradeoff analysis is the most important section. The interviewer doesn't expect you to design a perfect ERP platform in 45 minutes. They want to see that you understand the key tradeoffs and can make reasoned decisions under uncertainty.

Tradeoff 1: Consistency vs. Availability for Financial Close. The financial close requires strong consistency — you cannot report a balance that is stale. Therefore, the core ledger must use a CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance) data store. During a network partition, the system favors consistency over availability: if a regional data center is isolated, it stops accepting transactions until the partition is resolved. This is acceptable because of the regulatory requirement for accurate financial reporting. However, read-only queries can still be served from eventually consistent replicas during the partition.

Tradeoff 2: Modular Monolith vs. Microservices. As discussed in Section 4, the recommendation is modular monolith for the core ERP and microservices for edge capabilities. The key insight for the interview: acknowledge that this is NOT a binary choice. The hybrid approach captures the benefits of both patterns while mitigating their weaknesses. Name specific services that should be microservices (document management, analytics, workflow) and explain why.

Tradeoff 3: Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Integration. Cross-module business processes (Order-to-Cash) touch multiple modules. Should these be synchronous (API calls) or asynchronous (events)? Answer: Command-Query Separation. Queries (read data across modules) should be synchronous — a report must show current data. Commands (write operations) should be asynchronous with event-driven orchestration. The order creation request picks up inventory via async event, checks credit via async event, and notifies the customer via async event. The user gets an immediate acknowledgment; the full process completes within seconds.

Tradeoff 4: HANA vs. Open Source Database. HANA provides unmatched column-store performance for HTAP workloads, deep integration with CDS, and 10x compression for financial data. The cost is licensing ($10K+/TB/year) and vendor lock-in. Open source alternatives (PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB + ClickHouse) are cheaper and more flexible but require significantly more engineering effort to achieve equivalent performance. The recommendation for a production ERP at FAANG scale: start with PostgreSQL for data that doesn't need HTAP, and use HANA only for the core financial ledger where HTAP and compression matter most.

calculation.formula: TotalCost5yr = (DevCost x 1.3 overhead) + (InfraCost x 60 months) + MigrationCost

cp.monthlyPenalty: $14.4M — modular monolith: lower infra ($120K/mo) but higher coordination cost over 5 years

ap.monthlyPenalty: $18.2M — microservices: higher infra ($220K/mo for service mesh + observability) but lower coordination cost

calculation.breakeven: At 8+ services, the microservices TCO becomes cheaper because monolith coordination costs grow as O(n2) while microservices coordination grows as O(n). However, for ERP, the coordination cost is dwarfed by the consistency requirement — making the monolith the correct choice regardless of TCO.

rows: ERP data model too interconnected for sharding

details: Staff+ Insight: A global ERP system design question tests your ability to handle extreme complexity with constrained time. The interviewer doesn't expect a complete design. They want to see: (1) Requirements clarification before jumping to solution, (2) Explicit tradeoff analysis (CP vs AP, monolith vs microservices), (3) Knowledge of enterprise patterns without being stuck in SAP vocabulary. Practice structuring your answer: Requirements → Data Model → Architecture → Tradeoffs.
